data.title: Precision Drilling: Accelerating Capital Returns Should Put A Floor On The Stock
data.description:
All values are in CAD. Prices refer to the TSX traded stock and not the one on the US side.

In our inaugural coverage of Precision Drilling Corporation (TSX:PD:CA) (NYSE:PDS), we gave a brief history of the firm and went over why the current capital allocation plan had potential to deliver superb returns. Of course, the market decided that the company was still Precision Drilling and Quantum Drilling. This one actually had a 25% free cash flow yield today versus GAAP profitability based on 2040 estimates for Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR). So it made absolute sense for the market to say "meh" and deliver this for our buy thesis.

We review the Q4-2024 results and tell you why we expect our thesis to pan out even if we cannot convince the company to change its name to DeepSeek Drilling.

Q4-2024

While the company's overall trajectory remains excellent, Q4-2024 definitely showed a few soft spots. Revenues declined by 7.6% and the adjusted EBITDA missed estimates by a modest amount. Net capital spending was $50 million, a bit higher than where analysts expected.

The drilling market in the US came in softer than expected while Canada stayed relative resilient. You could see that in the stark contrast in the year over year delta in these two markets. Both drilling days and revenue per day moved in decidedly different directions. PDS also felt further pain as its operating costs on both sides of the 47th parallel rose year over year.

This reduced field margins to $14,559 per day, about 3% lower than their own guidance. In the face of these challenges, it was impressive that the company still delivered an overall solid EBITDA. But the analysts were quick to pounce on the miss and we saw a few downgrades and lower price targets post the quarter.

Outlook

PDS is not a get-rich-quick story. It is a longer-term deep value play where the company is working hard to fix the undervaluation. This begins with getting debt levels down.

As stated in their press release, they achieved exactly the midpoint of their 2024 target.

Reduce debt by between $150 million and $200 million and allocate 25% to 35% of free cash flow before debt repayments for share repurchases.

Reduced debt by $176 million and ended the year with a Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of approximately 1.4 times. On track to achieve a sustained Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of below 1.0 times.

Returned $75 million to shareholders through share repurchases, achieving the midpoint of our target range.

Renewed our NCIB in September, allowing repurchases of up to 10% of the public float.

Source: PDS Q4-2024 Press Release

The company announced its longer-term plans and the trajectory remains similar.

Reduce debt by at least $100 million in 2025 and debt by $700 million between 2022 and 2027, while remaining committed to achieving a sustained Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA ratio of below 1.0 times.

Allocate 35% to 45% of free cash flow, before debt repayments, directly to shareholders and continue moving direct shareholder capital returns toward 50% of free cash flow thereafter.

Source: PDS Q4-2024 Press Release

As we had previously stated, the initial round of buybacks have not materially moved the share count. That can be partially attributed to the figure below.

But reducing debt, reducing interest expense and a higher shareholder capital allocation, should change things. For 2025 the mean estimate is for about $300 million of free cash flow. We think this is a bit backward looking and focuses a lot on the Q4-2024 pain. Natural gas prices have improved in Q1-2025, thanks to persistent cold across North America. This should improve drilling at least modestly from here. Consensus is pricing a mild adjusted EBITDA decline. But let's run with their numbers and then knock of $50 million for stock-based compensation. We are still left with $250 million here relative to the following market capitalization.

The company should be able to return $125 million in the form of buybacks in 2025. This probably comes to an effective $80 million after accounting for stock-based compensation. The remaining cash would be directed towards debt reduction. So it might again be a bit from a stock price movement perspective. After all, something trading this cheap can get cheaper. But the numbers get really good once you go past 2025 and will likely get incredible if we have a genuine drilling up cycle. We could easily see $400 million of free cash flow in a peak year. Valuation can really swing and give you phenomenal returns. Imagine a 5-10X free cash flow multiple at the peak, and account for the reduced share count over the next two years.

Verdict

PDS is not the most efficient user of assets and that is partially the reason for the multiples relative to Nabors Industries (NBR) and Patterson-UTI Energy Inc. (PTEN).

But the discount here is phenomenal from multiple angles. You have the massive free cash flow yield which goes more and more towards shareholder returns over time. But even from sheer worth of these assets, you are getting a bargain. Realistically, we think tangible book value understates replacement cost by about 20%-30%. This is because real loss of value is far less than booked depreciation. So a true NAV like metric here would be closer to $150. The stock is trading at close to 2.5X EV to EBITDA. In a world where 50x sales seems to be a "bargain", one cannot rule out further value compression in actual value stocks. But we are sticking with this and adding positions married to December 2025 covered calls.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Celsius Holdings: Stock Surged 30% Post Earnings, Deep Dive Into The Numbers
data.description:
Introduction

Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) announced its Q4 2024 earnings report today after the close. In the after-hour trading session, the stock first sold off quite substantially, down 9%. This was after The Wall Street Journal spread the story that CELH was buying its rival “Alani Nu” for $1.8 billion. Shortly after this, CELH presented earnings and made public that they were indeed buying Alani Nu. The stock is currently up by 30%, just a few minutes after the earnings release.

I will give you a brief look over the earnings and my opinion on them.

Q4 2024 earnings report

Solid 2024 growth, even though the last quarter saw increased pressure due to declining sales in North America.

Financial highlights of the fourth quarter:

CELH reported $332.2 million in sales, which are down 4% compared to the same quarter in the previous year. This is mainly due to North American sales being down 6% YOY. With $311.9 million, the North American region represents the major source of revenue. Contrary to this is CELH's international business, which increased by 39% YOY to $20.3 million. This was done via the expansion into the UK, Ireland, France, Australia, and New Zealand.

Thanks to lower freight and material costs, the gross margin improved by 240 basis points to 50.2%. Contrary to this, net income decreased by a whopping -138% to -$18.9 million. According to CELH, this stems from high legal expenses, restructuring costs, and co-packing obligations.

  • Revenue: $332.2 million (-4%)
  • Gross Margin: 50.2% (+240 basis points)
  • Net Income: -$18.9 million (-138%)
  • SG&A Expenses: $185.2 million (+73%)
  • Diluted EPS: -$0.11 (previous year: $0.17).

Full-Year 2024: Moderate Growth Despite Challenges

Even though the fourth quarter dragged sales growth down, CELH managed to achieve 3% revenue growth, reaching $1.36 billion for the full year. International sales were the main driver with a 37% increase for 2024. However, net income declined by 36% to $145.1 million, and diluted EPS fell 42% to $0.45. According to the management, investments in vertical integration, new market expansion, and strategic initiatives impacted short-term profitability but will drive long-term growth.

Key Figures (2024 vs. 2023):

  • Revenue: $1.36 billion (+3%)
  • International Revenue: $74.7 million (+37%)
  • Gross Margin: 50.2% (+220 basis points)
  • Net Income: $145.1 million (-36%)
  • Diluted EPS: $0.45 (-42%)
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $255.7 million (-13%).

Market Performance

While Celsius’ U.S. retail sales grew by 22% for the year, the company experienced a slight decline in market share in Q4 (-0.5% to 10.9%). Nevertheless, for the full year, Celsius' market share increased by 1.6% to 11.8%.

CEO John Fieldly highlighted the company’s strong brand momentum and long-term potential:

”Our record $1.36 billion revenue underscores the strength of our brand. With the Alani Nu acquisition and continued expansion, we are well-positioned for sustained growth.”

CFO Jarrod Langhans added:

”Our financial strategy enables us to balance sustainable growth with profitable expansion. Investments in vertical integration and international markets provide long-term competitive advantages.”

Celsius Holdings: 2025 Outlook – Growth, Expansion & Strategic Moves

According to management, Celsius Holdings is ready for strong growth despite short-term challenges. Focus areas for 2025 are international expansion, product innovation, retail growth, and the Alani Nu acquisition.

Because the outlook and strategies are quite extensive, I split them in different segments to give you a better overview.

Alani Nu Acquisition: A Key Growth Driver

The $1.65 billion acquisition of Alani Nu is expected to:

  • Expand market share with strong Gen Z and millennial appeal
  • Contribute $595 million in additional revenue
  • Generate $50 million in annual cost synergies
  • Strengthen presence in functional lifestyle products.

Goal: $2 billion+ in revenue through brand integration.

International Expansion: Scaling Global Presence

  • +37% international sales in 2024 – major focus area for 2025
  • Leverage PepsiCo’s distribution network for faster global scaling
  • Targeted growth regions: Asia-Pacific, Europe.

Market potential: Global energy drinks sector to grow 10% CAGR (2024-2029).

Product Innovation & Portfolio Expansion

Consumer demand for health-conscious and functional beverages is rising. Celsius plans to:

  • Launch new product categories: hydration, protein-based drinks
  • Introduce limited-edition flavors to increase consumer engagement
  • Expand into new functional wellness segments.

Goal: Stay ahead of market trends and reinforce brand loyalty.

Margin Expansion & Profitability Improvements

Despite higher SG&A expenses, Celsius aims to:

  • Optimize supply chains for lower material & freight costs
  • Enhance economies of scale through expanded production
  • Improve direct-to-consumer (DTC) sales for higher margins.

Target: Maintain 50%+ gross margin while investing in growth.

Retail & Distribution Growth in North America

  • 37% increase in retail distribution points in 2024
  • Strengthen partnerships with Walmart, Target, and major retailers
  • Expand into fitness centers & health-focused outlets
  • Boost marketing and brand visibility to regain sales momentum.

Objective: Regain U.S. sales growth despite the Q4 slowdown.

Financial Strategy & Liquidity Management

  • $890 million in cash reserves ensures flexibility
  • Low net leverage (~1.0x) even after Alani Nu deal
  • Balanced capital allocation: Growth investments + debt reduction.

Plan: Maintain financial strength while pursuing aggressive expansion.

2025 Key Targets

  • $2 billion+ total revenue
  • 50 million in cost synergies from the Alani Nu integration
  • Double-digit international sales growth
  • Improved gross margin via operational efficiencies
  • Expanded retail footprint & new product launches.

Previous financial performance

CELH’s recent quarterly numbers look nearly perfect. Revenue, EPS, and free cash flow have all grown substantially since 2022, and they managed to become comfortably profitable. The last quarter presents an exception, as you can see in the following chart:

As with everything in the market, there are always ups and downs; occasional bumps are normal. What matters is the long-term trend. And I would argue that the long-term trend looks good. In my opinion, the recent earnings have shown that the long-term growth path is secured.

Valuation

Currently, CELH is valued at 32x forward earnings, far below its three-year average and near its lowest historical valuation.

For a company with CELH’s growth, market position, total addressable market (TAM), and excellent management, I believe this valuation is justified.

After the very positive earnings price reaction, the valuation will be higher. But this is the forward P/E, and I am sure analysts will raise their estimates, lowering the valuation again. In any case, it will still be way above its previous fwd P/E average.

Risks

The major risk I see is that inflation is further accelerating. For CELH this would create two major concerns:

  • higher inflation data could force the FED to raise rates again, which would increase the cost of financing, which hits smaller growth companies the hardest.
  • accelerating inflation means an even higher cost of living, which is already a problem for most people, especially in the US. CELH sells something that is not necessary and could and will be ditched by consumers if they have to choose between things they need and CELH's energy drinks.

CELH’s management has not addressed this, but I see it as a risk.

Conclusion

CELH is entering a pivotal phase in its growth journey. Short-term profitability challenges exist, but the company’s strong brand momentum, global expansion, and strategic acquisitions position it for accelerated growth in 2025 and beyond. CELH’s management has proven that they can steer the company toward growth and are among the top in the industry. While there are risks and short-term profitability challenges, I am sure that the current stock prices will be a steal compared to the prices you will have to pay soon. Therefore, I rate this stock as a buy.

data.title: Mister Car Wash: Quarterly Results Demonstrate That Upside Is Warranted
data.description:
February 20th was an interesting day for shareholders of Mister Car Wash, Inc. (NASDAQ:MCW). As the company's name suggests, it is an owner and operator of a network of car washes. This may not seem like the most exciting space for investors to buy into. However, I consider it fascinating. Well, during the day, shares of the company closed down 1.6% after trading higher by as much as 10.5%.

This came after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year and provided guidance for 2025. I view the data provided by management as positive. However, shares are currently down 6.3% since I last reaffirmed the company as a “buy” candidate in November 2024. Over that same window of time, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 3.5%.

Even though earnings per share fell short of analysts’ expectations, revenue and adjusted earnings per share exceeded what analysts anticipated. Furthermore, guidance for 2025 is encouraging. To be honest with you, I find the firm's underperformance to be perplexing. The fact of the matter is that shares are quite attractively priced. And when you add on top of this future growth prospects, the business still makes sense as a “buy” candidate to me.

A decent quarter

All things considered, I would say that the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year was a good time for shareholders of Mister Car Wash. Revenue for the company came in at $251.2 million. That represents an increase of 9.2% compared to the $230.1 million the company reported just one year earlier. This revenue figure ended up being $2.7 million more than what analysts were hoping to see. One contributor to this increase was a rise in the number of locations that the company has in operation, from 476 last year to 514 this year. However, the business also enjoyed a 6% increase in comparable store sales.

One thing that is certainly helping the business from a growth perspective is what it calls its UWC (Unlimited Wash Club) memberships. This subscription plan gives customers the ability to wash their vehicles as often as they would like, at any of the firm's locations. These are month-to-month subscriptions that create recurring revenue for the company and that also allow customers to skip the line with certain priority member lanes at locations. For one random city that I selected, Monroe, Michigan, I noticed that the plans range between $19.99 per month and $39.99 per month. Obviously, the plan you choose determines the different features that you are entitled to.

Management estimates that these members spend, on average, about four times more with the average retail car wash consumer does. So in addition to revenue being recurring, it is also much more significant for the company. This subscription base grew from 2.08 million in the final quarter of 2023 to 2.12 million in the final quarter of 2024. From the third quarter to the fourth quarter, the firm added 14,000 members on a net basis. Furthermore, these members went from comprising 74% of the firm's revenue in the final quarter of 2023 to comprising 75% last year.

On the bottom line, the picture was mixed, but generally positive. Earnings per share actually contracted from $0.04 to $0.03, taking net profits down from $12.4 million to $9.2 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.03 below what analysts were hoping to see. This was primarily because of a surge in the loss on the sale of assets from $3.6 million to $13 million. But on an adjusted basis, earnings per share went from $0.07 to $0.09, pushing adjusted net profits for the company up from $24 million to $30.7 million. The adjusted earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.02 greater than what analysts anticipated. To be honest with you, I'm not a huge fan of the company including stock-based compensation (“SBC”) into this calculation. But I understand why they did it.

Other profitability metrics improved on a year-over-year basis, without exception. Operating cash flow, for instance, grew from $39.2 million to $49.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $56.5 million to $67.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $69.5 million to $78.3 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for 2024 as a whole relative to 2023. A new increased nicely, driven not only by the aforementioned increase in location count, but also because of a 3% rise in comparable store sales. Even though net profits declined, all other profitability metrics for the company improved year over year.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management said that revenue should climb even higher from the $994.7 million reported in 2024 between $1.038 billion and $1.064 billion. This rise should be driven by continued expansion, as well as comparable store sales increases of between 1% and 3%. Management also claimed that adjusted net profits should be between $141 million and $149 million, while EBITDA should come in between $334 million and $346 million. Both of these would represent improvements over what the company achieved last year. This kind of increase would likely translate to a rise in adjusted operating cash flow to $289 million.

It is worth mentioning that the company's current guidance is based on capital expenditures that should be scaled back from what was allocated in 2024. In 2024, the company spent $330.1 million on capital projects. But for 2025, that number is expected to be between $275 million and $305 million. Part of this reduction is because of the firm's plan to engage in only $40 million to $50 million of sale leaseback transactions. This compares to the $134.9 million engaged in during 2024. For those not familiar, a sale leaseback transaction is a case where a company sells off assets to another firm and then leases those back, usually on a long-term basis. This gives the company significant upfront cash with which to reinvest in growth. When done right, it can be an affordable way to expand. But if it's not done right, it can cause higher operational expenses over the long run.

With the $275 million to $305 million in capital spending, the company intends to allocate between $225 million and $250 million on new locations. This will help open between 30 and 35 new car washes. The remaining $50 million to $55 million in spending will be dedicated to store level maintenance, productivity improvements, and the continued integration of locations that are acquired instead of built from scratch.

In the chart above, you can see what the stock looks like from a valuation standpoint based on these figures. Shares are at the high end of what I would consider to be undervalued. In addition to being attractively priced compared to cash flows, the stock also looks cheap, compared to what it has been in the past, on a price to book basis. Driven by an increase in book value per share from $2.35 in 2021 to $3.02 in 2024, and by a plunge in the firm's share price, its price to book multiple has dropped from 7.75 to 2.57. This trend can be seen in the chart below.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I would argue that Mister Car Wash remains an interesting prospect. I am disappointed by how shares have performed since my last article. However, the stock is attractively priced on an absolute basis and, on a price to book basis, relative to what it was over the past few years. Add on top of this continued growth on its top and bottom lines, and I have no problem keeping the firm a “buy” at this time.

data.title: Broadcom Is Threatening AMD's Data Center Opportunity
data.description:
Real men have fabs. - AMD founder Jerry Sanders.

For Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD), losing its fabs that its founder cherished so much appears to have been an enormous blessing in disguise. Ironically, the company now has no fabs and is led by brilliant CEO Lisa Su. It is worth mentioning that, with deep computer science and electrical engineer knowledge, including three MIT degrees, she is probably more talented and capable than any of the recent CEOs at its main competitor Intel (INTC). The amount of shareholder value created by AMD in the past ten years is jaw-dropping, as is the value destruction that has taken place at Intel.

All of this to say that AMD has benefited from its rivals’ missteps, a capable and visionary CEO, but also from the forced decision to give up its fabs. Having to let go of its chip manufacturing operations because of its precarious financial position at the time was perhaps the best thing that could have happened to AMD. It then was forced to search for a manufacturing partner in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited, aka TSMC (TSM). This in turn meant that it was able to free-ride on TSMC's cutting-edge manufacturing capabilities and deliver more advanced silicon without having to pay for all the necessary R&D and CapEx required.

The technology development cost was therefore spread between the multiple TSMC customers, and they have been sharing the rewards in the form of privileged access to cutting-edge nodes. This has meant that for the same design, AMD got better performance and energy efficiency compared to Intel.

Unfortunately for AMD, this long-term tailwind could be coming to an end. Intel has started using TSMC as a foundry for certain products such as the Lunar Lake processor, also known as the Core Ultra 200V series, which is being fully manufactured by TSMC. Further complicating things for AMD, Broadcom (AVGO) is reportedly considering a bid for Intel's chip design business, letting someone else take the fabs. A new fab-less Intel as part of Broadcom would free the business to fully take advantage of working with TSMC, and erase AMD's manufacturing competitive advantage of working with TSMC.

Weakening Competitive Moat

Broadcom is spoiling AMD's party in additional ways, including helping Nvidia's (NVDA) customers design their own artificial intelligence (AI) chips. Many AMD investors were hoping AMD GPUs would be the Pepsi (PEP) to Nvidia's Coca-Cola (KO). Unfortunately for AMD investors, it is increasingly looking like it will be a similar situation to Starbucks (SBUX) where the main competition is homemade coffee.

In a recent Bloomberg interview, Lisa Su sounds very confident for the most part. However, she struggled to come up with a good reply when Emily Chang asks her if the fact that many of her customers are now designing their own AI chips is a major threat for AMD. Broadcom has been helping many of these customers design their own chips. Even the likes of OpenAI and ByteDance (BDNCE) are talking to Broadcom to get help designing their own proprietary AI chips. The reason that they are partnering with Broadcom is because of their deep chip design knowledge and vast intellectual property. Broadcom has one of the broadest intellectual property portfolios in the industry, with roughly 21,000 patents.

Part of AMD's moat also rests on its x86 license, which it was able to obtain from Intel when IBM (IBM) said they required at least two CPU suppliers. Intel reluctantly licensed x86 to AMD, but the architecture is now becoming less relevant as mobile phones and tablets tend to use Arm Holdings' (ARM) architecture, with Apple (AAPL) even switching its Macs to an ARM-based architecture recently. Unlike with x86, AMD does not have any major competitive advantage when using ARM's architecture versus rivals like Qualcomm (QCOM) and MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF). Reportedly, there are more than 60 laptop designs from OEMs currently in production or development based on the Snapdragon X series. Microsoft hedged its x86 dependence by developing a Windows version for ARM devices, and professional reviews for Windows laptops using ARM-based CPUs like Qualcomm's Snapdragon X have a stark warning for AMD:

This is the fiercest Microsoft has been able to compete with MacBooks in price, performance, and battery life, and while Qualcomm’s Snapdragon chips don’t outright beat Apple’s M3 chip (with an eight-core CPU and 10-core GPU) in every single one of our benchmarks, they could make Intel and AMD scramble to catch up to another competitor — this time, on their home turf.

Financials

Broadcom's strategy appears to be paying off, as its revenue has inflected higher while AMD's appears to be decelerating. AMD's $25.8 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2024 was only 14% higher compared to the previous year, even though there was an improvement in the fourth quarter to a ~24% growth rate. This is still significantly slower compared to Broadcom's current growth rate, and Broadcom is also delivering higher gross margins.

AMD delivered $1.0 in GAAP earnings per share (EPS) during fiscal year 2024, and $3.31 in Non-GAAP EPS. We would caution against relying too much on the Non-GAAP figure, given that a major reason for the discrepancy is due to $0.86 in stock-based compensation (“SBC”), which we consider to be a very real expense even if it is non-cash. Looking at the different business segments, Data center and Client continue to perform well, both growing significantly year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter. Gaming is showing signs of bottoming out, as it was significantly down year-over-year, but up quarter-over-quarter. Meanwhile, the Embedded business continues to show poor performance, although the decline rate appears to be moderating.

Stock-based Compensation

For a company with a GAAP net profit margin of ~6%, we believe its stock-based compensation expense of roughly 4.4% of quarterly revenue to be excessive. Since 2022 the gross amount of stock-based compensation has more than tripled from ~$400 million to close to $1.4 billion in the trailing twelve months.

Over the past ten years, average diluted shares outstanding have roughly doubled. Ironically, many companies add-back SBC arguing that it is a non-cash expense, but then spend billions repurchasing shares. This is the case with AMD, announcing in February 2022 an $8 billion share repurchase authorization, yet this has barely been able to offset the impact of stock-based compensation, as can be seen in the graph below.

Danger of Leveraged AMD ETFs

Still, we agree that AMD is a well run technology company and would not bet against it, and can understand why some investors continue to invest. Where we see enormous risk is with the leveraged ETFs on AMD's daily returns, such as the GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF (AMDL). Long-term investors should pay attention to the volatility drag effect, which means that capital can be destroyed even if the underlying shares deliver a positive return. Below is a hypothetical example showing how AMD shares could end up delivering a slight positive return after a few days of high volatility, while the leveraged ETF would have lost money.

Day 1 (-10%) Day 2 (-20%) Day 3 (+20%) Day 4 (+20%)
AMD 0.9 0.72 0.86 1.03
AMD 2x Long ETF 0.8 0.48 0.67 0.94

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-stock ETFs such as AMDL. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Outlook

AMD's guidance for the first quarter of fiscal year 2025 is for revenue of roughly $7.1 billion, which at the mid-point would represent growth of ~30% compared to the first quarter of fiscal year 2024. While this is an impressive growth rate, it is about a third of Nvidia's recent growth rate, meaning that the company is losing market share. Looking further ahead, we believe that growth is likely to further decelerate as many current customers begin transitioning to their proprietary solutions.

Valuation

We find AMD's valuation difficult to justify, with a GAAP Price/Earnings multiple in the triple digits, an EV/EBITDA of ~34x, and a market cap exceeding 7 times its revenue.

Compared to the information technology sector, AMD appears expensive on most valuation metrics when using the GAAP version. For instance, the forward Price/Earnings multiple is almost 50% higher compared to the sector median. Other relevant valuation metrics, such as the forward EV/Sales ratio, is roughly 70% above the sector median. With FY2024 revenue growth of only 14%, and a single-digit GAAP net profit margin, it is difficult to justify such a wide premium.

Investors should be aware that the current valuation for most of the companies involved with AI are discounting enormous growth long into the future, and prices could decline significantly even with minor growth disappointments. AMD only appears reasonably valued when compared to peers like ARM.

Risks

The biggest risk we see for AMD currently is the stretched valuation, even after shares have retreated from previous highs. The company is still growing revenue at a fast pace, but at a lower rate than competitors like Nvidia and Broadcom. Its Gaming and Embedded segments are also delivering relatively poor performance, and competition will probably intensify in its Client segment with ARM-based laptops.

In the medium to long term, the biggest risk we see for AMD is that many of its current customers, with the help of Broadcom and other chip specialists, switch to proprietary devices. We also see a significant risk to AMD should Broadcom acquire the design part of Intel, as it could move production to TSMC and eliminate one of AMD's advantages.

Conclusion

Broadcom is helping AMD clients develop their custom AI chips, which could impact the company's best performing business segment. The company's valuation is very high, especially when stock-based compensation is considered. While the company has delivered strong growth, and continues growing at a rapid pace, we see multiple risks. Many of them are the result of Broadcom, and AMD customers' desire to move to their proprietary designs, as well as the risk that Broadcom could buy Intel's chip design business and move manufacturing to TSMC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ON Holding: A Decent Buy Signal After The Recent Pullback For This Swiss Sportswear Company
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The stock of ON Holding AG (NYSE:ONON) is quite new on the New York Stock Exchange, going public on September 15th 2021, with an opening price of $35.40.

Since then, the movement of the price has been marked by two distinct phases: Low volume liquidations during the first 18 months, which led ONON to lose more than 50% of its value, effectively shaking out impatient short-term speculators, and strong buying interest in the following period up to today, with significantly increased trading volumes and the stock recording gains of +140% (+52% from the IPO), reaching historic highs.

The prevailing two-year uptrend appears very healthy, especially when considering the consistently strong upward movement of key financial metrics, which we will examine below, and I find it likely that this trend will continue.

The recent -21% pullback presents a repositioning opportunity, with an initial target of $74 representing a +45% potential gain.

Company Overview

On Holding AG is a Swiss company operating in the athletic apparel industry, with a focus on running shoes and sportswear. Despite being relatively “young” (founded in 2010), it quickly gained popularity by introducing an innovative technology (CloudTec) while at the same time, its differentiated business model (using direct-to-consumer DTC channels and targeting premium consumers willing to pay for high-performance footwear, unlike budget-friendly competitors) allowed it to stand out among industry giants like Nike and Adidas.

The team of Martin Hoffman and Marc Maurer has achieved significant market share growth through controlled, multi-channel expansion, with a strong focus on the active sports community. Equally impressive is the expansion of its geographic footprint, primarily driven by its online platform, where the number of members has skyrocketed (multiplying ninefold since its listing on the NYSE).

Finally, ON’s products are available in all major existing retail networks (footlocker, Dick’s, bloomingdale’s, Hype etc.) confirming their widespread acceptance by consumers worldwide.

The above factors have led the Company to steady growth over the years, demonstrating its strong foundation and long-term potential for stability and further evolution.

Solid financials, high profit margins

Before diving into a technical analysis of ONON’s stock, let’s first take a look at its financial position, focusing on three key aspects.

What stands out to me the most is its remarkable ability to grow both in terms of revenue and earnings, despite operating in an intensely competitive industry, facing well-established giants like Nike (NYSE:NKE) and Adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY).

Attempting a direct comparison with these Companies ($109 billion vs $17 billion) would be futile, but evaluating ON individually, I see a fundamentally strong company with solid characteristics and a strong likelihood of continuing to gain market share while improving its financials, further proof of the broad acceptance of its products.

The figures are impressive and ONON appears to outperform the entire sector in revenue, EBITDA and EPS growth, even when compared to the two industry giants.

I expect ONON to continue gaining market share, which suggests that its stock has significant upside potential in the medium to long-term. This assumption is based primarily on its (so far) successful business model and its competitive advantages. My focus is on three main points:

  • Omnichannel Distribution Strategy - leveraging direct-to-consumer DTC channels, premium retail partnerships and digital sales.
  • Premium Pricing Strategy- targeting premium consumers willing to pay for high performance footwear, unlike budget-friendly competitors.
  • Innovative Footwear Technology- including CloudTec technology, Speedboard innovation and the CyclonlLine.

In November 2024, ON reached record sales and profit margins and raised its FY2024 guidance, expecting sales growth (as of March 4) of at least 32% and a gross profit margin increase of 100bps.

The second key point worth noting is the efficient way ONON converts strong revenue into income, as its net income margin is estimated to be just below 6%, compared to the sector’s weighted average of 4.5%.

This figure alone is meaningful, but more importantly, it reinforces my confidence that the expected growth we discussed earlier will also translate into strong profitability. This is why I believe the stock's upward momentum is unlikely to slow down anytime soon.

The third key point worth highlighting is ON’s cash flow performance. Firstly, its operating cash flow has seen remarkable improvement, currently estimated at $547 million, compared to a five-year average of $62 million (having been negative until mid-2023). Even more striking is its exceptionally high LFCF Margin of 15.45% (net cash flow available for distribution after covering interest payments and tax obligations), which is three times higher than the average of the sector.

Looking the Company as a whole, it is clear that ONON is a highly promising case. While it lacks the long-standing track record of more established industry players, the available data is undeniably impressive and its product is objectively strong.

Risks: High valuation, strong competition

Now let’s look at the downsides. Unsurprisingly, ONON’s valuation is high as the stock trades near its all-time highs, at 114 times its earnings and 76 times its FWD earnings. This presents a significant challenge both when compared to the broader sector (where ONON trades at five times the average of the sector) and when looking at other valuation metrics, such as P/CF (30x TTM, 73x FWD), despite the Company’s exceptionally strong performance in these areas.

This elevated valuation acts as a deterrent for potential investors, who may hesitate to buy at such a high premium, even after the recent 21% pullback, which naturally reduced the P multiple. It is also important to remember that, despite the Company’s strong growth, this high valuation could indicate that market expectations are already priced in to some extent.

Additionally, the most significant risk is ONON’s future. As discussed earlier, the company is currently fully focused on growth (and is executing exceptionally well), aggressively capturing market share from well-established competitors. For now, it is succeeding due to the product differentiation we have highlighted, but maintaining this trajectory in the long run will be a key challenge.

There is still one key question: What triggered the recent -21% pullback? A primary explanation is the anticipation and eventual downgrade of ONON by Goldman Sachs, which revised its rating on On Holding AG from “Buy” to “Neutral”, while maintaining a $57 price target. This decision was based on concerns over increasing competition in the athletic footwear market and signs of slowing growth in the U.S.

Therefore, for someone to invest at this point, they would need to be convinced (as I am) that these factors will not have a lasting impact on the stock, preventing further selling pressure.

It is also worth noting that while the company has disappointed in the last two quarters, it traditionally delivers strong positive surprises in Q1, driven by seasonal trends.

Stock Overview: Strong upward momentum and an interesting buy signal

The stock is clearly in an uptrend. The investment community has already begun to believe that this “young” company will continue gaining market share, while the strong profit margins we analyzed earlier further reinforce its position.

The recent pullback from all-time highs has, at last for now, found support at $51, a level from which I expect a strong upward reaction in the short term. If momentum holds, this could pave the way for a new rally towards $74, setting fresh all-time highs. This target aligns with the upper boundary of the current bullish movement.

Momentum remains very strong and the +45% upside potential to my target is attractive enough for me to take the trade, despite the elevated valuation. After all, looking at the stock’s behavior over the past few years, pullbacks have been frequent and each has proven valuable in the bigger picture.

Its liquidity is more than sufficient, as an average of 1% of shares outstanding are traded daily, with a very tight bid-ask spread of approximately $0.1.

Conclusion

ONON is a highly promising and relatively young $17 billion company in the footwear industry, with strong and healthy financials.

The widespread consumer acceptance of its products and its business model justify its successful movement so far.

Its stock has been following a high-quality uptrend, with strong prospects for continuation, potentially reaching new all-time highs.

The recent pullback has created a repositioning opportunity, with an initial upside potential of 45%.
